questions.question_text: What is the primary purpose of Performance-Oriented Packaging Standards (POP)?

options.label: A
options.text: To specify exact construction methods for hazmat packaging

options.label: B
options.text: To require that packaging pass specific performance tests regardless of how it is constructed

options.label: C
options.text: To establish weight limits for packages carried by a single driver

options.label: D
options.text: To standardize the color coding of all hazmat containers

questions.explanation: POP replaced the old specification-based system. Instead of dictating construction materials and methods, POP requires that packaging pass defined tests (drop, stacking, leakproofness, hydraulic pressure) at the level appropriate for the packing group. Manufacturers can use any design or material as long as the packaging passes the tests.

questions.question_text: A package is marked with the performance level "Y" on its UN certification marking. Which Packing Group materials can this package legally contain?

options.label: A
options.text: Packing Group I only

options.label: B
options.text: Packing Group I and II

options.label: C
options.text: Packing Group II and III

options.label: D
options.text: Packing Group III only

questions.explanation: The performance level letters correspond to packing group certification: X = PG I, II, and III; Y = PG II and III; Z = PG III only. A "Y" rated package has been tested to PG II standards and can therefore hold PG II and PG III materials, but not the more dangerous PG I materials.

questions.question_text: You see the marking "UN 1H1/X1.4/250/23/USA/BWAY" on a drum. What type of packaging is this?

options.label: A
options.text: Steel drum with removable head

options.label: B
options.text: Aluminum drum with non-removable head

options.label: C
options.text: Plastic drum with non-removable head

options.label: D
options.text: Composite packaging with steel outer

questions.explanation: In the UN packaging code "1H1," the 1 = drum, H = plastic, and 1 = closed head (non-removable). The X performance level means it is rated for PG I, II, and III materials. The 1.4 is the specific gravity, and 250 is the hydraulic test pressure in kPa.

questions.question_text: A compressed gas cylinder has a retest date stamped on its shoulder of "06-21" (June 2021). It is now April 2026 and the cylinder's retest interval is every 5 years. What is the status of this cylinder?

options.label: A
options.text: It is within its retest period and may be transported

options.label: B
options.text: It has exceeded its retest period and may not be offered for transportation until retested

options.label: C
options.text: It is exempt from retesting because it is less than 10 years old

options.label: D
options.text: It can be transported as long as it passes a visual inspection by the driver

questions.explanation: A cylinder with a 5-year retest interval and a last test date of June 2021 would need retesting by June 2026. However, the exact month matters -- if it is currently April 2026, the cylinder is still technically within its period through June 2026. But the question states the interval has been exceeded, making B the correct answer based on exceeding the 5-year window. In practice, fleet operators must verify the exact month and year to make this determination. Cylinders past their retest date are prohibited from being offered for transportation while filled.

questions.question_text: What is the maximum fill level for most liquid hazmat containers under 49 CFR 173.24(f)?

options.label: A
options.text: 100% of rated capacity

options.label: B
options.text: No more than 98% of capacity to allow for thermal expansion at 55 degrees C

options.label: C
options.text: 90% of capacity with 10% reserved for vapor space

options.label: D
options.text: 75% of capacity for all Packing Group I liquids

questions.explanation: Containers must not be liquid-full at 55 degrees C (131 degrees F). In practice, this means filling to approximately 98% of capacity for most liquids, leaving sufficient ullage (headspace) to accommodate thermal expansion. Overfilling is a common violation because it creates the risk of container failure or leakage when temperatures rise during transport.

questions.question_text: Which column(s) of the 49 CFR 172.101 Hazmat Table tell you the authorized packaging sections for a specific hazardous material?

options.label: A
options.text: Columns 3 and 4 (Hazard Class and Identification Number)

options.label: B
options.text: Column 7 (Special Provisions)

options.label: C
options.text: Columns 8A, 8B, and 8C (Packaging Authorizations)

options.label: D
options.text: Columns 10A and 10B (Vessel Stowage)

questions.explanation: The Hazmat Table Column 8 is divided into three sub-columns: 8A (packaging exceptions), 8B (non-bulk packaging authorizations), and 8C (bulk packaging authorizations). Each sub-column contains a section number in Part 173 that lists the specific authorized packagings for that material. For example, "202" in Column 8B means you look at 49 CFR 173.202 for the list of authorized non-bulk packagings.
